---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## A Clean DiD on Real Fair-Lending Data

**Learning objectives** — by the end you can:

- Pull and aggregate real HMDA into a county-year panel
- State a fair-lending DiD spec in full discipline
- Build a staggered synthetic panel with a known effect
- Recover that effect with Callaway–Sant'Anna where TWFE fails

::: notes
Welcome to the Week Four lab, part one of ten. This is the capstone of the week, and it is the first time you point the causal-inference machinery at a real fair-lending dataset that millions of mortgage applications actually live in. Chapter four-point-one built difference-in-differences from four cell means up to two-way fixed effects and the event study. Chapter four-point-two warned that the same regression becomes a contaminated weighted average once treatment timing is staggered, and it handed you the cure: Callaway and Sant'Anna's group-time average treatment effects. This lab puts both chapters to work.
:::

## The Mission

- Capstone of Week 4 — DiD on **real** mortgage data
- Source: HMDA Loan Application Register (public)
- Same data family as **Gao and Sun, 2019, PNAS**
- Build a county-year panel of **denial rates**
- Treatment = a staggered **state fair-lending** rule change
- Run the full pipeline, then stress parallel trends

::: notes
The dataset is the Home Mortgage Disclosure Act Loan Application Register — the public record of nearly every mortgage application filed in the United States. It is the same family of data that Professor Gao and his coauthor used to ask whether same-sex couples were treated differently when they applied to borrow, in Gao and Sun, two thousand nineteen, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. You will build a county-year panel of mortgage denial rates, define treatment by a state-level fair-lending regulatory change that different states adopt in different years — staggered adoption, exactly the Chapter four-point-two setting — and run the full pipeline. Two-way fixed effects first, so you feel why it is suspect here. Then the event study to inspect pre-trends. Then Callaway and Sant'Anna with clean controls to get an honest number. You stress parallel trends deliberately, the way a regulator or a referee would.
:::

## Two Paths Through the Lab

- **Path A — real HMDA**: the CFPB Data Browser API
- A reproducible *recipe* against a pinned data vintage
- We never fabricate specific HMDA numbers
- **Path B — synthetic fallback**: runs anywhere, no network
- A panel with a **known** planted effect
- Read both even if you only run one

::: notes
The lab gives you two paths, and you should read both even if you only run one. Path A is real HMDA — the public CFPB Data Browser application programming interface and the FFIEC bulk files. You see the exact query pattern, the schema you get back, and how to aggregate the raw application-level register up to a county-year panel of denial rates. We do not fabricate specific HMDA numbers; the real register is enormous and its values are what they are, so Path A is a reproducible recipe you run on the camp container against a pinned data vintage. Path B is a self-contained, seeded, synthetic county-year panel that looks like aggregated HMDA but is built with a known treatment effect. It runs anywhere, with no credentials and no network, and lets you run the entire pipeline and recover the effect you planted — confirming the estimator works before you trust it on data whose truth you cannot see.
:::

## The Discipline This Lab Drills

- When unsure a tool works, **build a universe where you know the truth**
- Path B is that universe; Path A is the messy world
- A real estimate is only as good as your demonstration
- Goals: pull and aggregate, state the spec, recover the truth

::: notes
The discipline this lab drills is the one the whole week builds toward and the one Lab Three stated as a slogan: when you are unsure whether a tool does what it claims, build a universe where you know the truth and check. Path B is that universe; Path A is the messy world you check it against. A causal estimate on real HMDA is only as trustworthy as your demonstration, on synthetic data, that the same code recovers a known answer. By the end you will pull and aggregate real HMDA, state a fair-lending DiD design in full spec discipline, build a staggered synthetic panel with a known effect, run two-way fixed effects, read an event study, and estimate Callaway and Sant'Anna group-time effects that recover the truth.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
This lab points the DiD machinery at **real fair-lending data** — HMDA denial rates under a staggered state policy. Path A is the messy real recipe; Path B is the synthetic universe where you know the truth and can verify the estimator. Next: pulling and shaping real HMDA.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: this lab is the Week Four capstone, pointing difference-in-differences at real fair-lending data — county-year mortgage denial rates under a staggered state policy, the same data family as Gao and Sun, two thousand nineteen. Path A is the messy real-world recipe against a pinned HMDA vintage; Path B is the synthetic universe where you plant a known effect and verify the estimator. In part two we begin Path A — pinning the HMDA vintage and querying the CFPB Data Browser.
:::
